refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ customer-defined target (default 0.999). Verification method: automated telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain redundant communication paths to achieve target availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions for availability audit.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication link with best-effort reliability (no redundancy)
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + secondary failover) with automatic switchover logic
DP-C: Mesh network topology with multi-hop redundancy and dynamic routing
DP-D: Hybrid approach: primary cellular + secondary satellite link with intelligent arbitration
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + secondary failover) with automatic switchover logic and continuous link health monitoring.

potential_sources: DP-B couples to FR-NGHE-010.1 (link loss detection) and FR-NGHE-010.2 (safe state transition) via failover trigger logic.
DP-B couples to network infrastructure selection (cellular vs. satellite vs. hybrid) which is a separate design decision.
DP-B does not strongly couple to vehicle dynamics or safety services beyond the SafetyAlert interface.
coupling_check.notes: DP-B is decoupled from specific physical transport medium; it is a logical architecture pattern applicable across multiple network technologies. Coupling is primarily to link monitoring and failover logic, which are contained within the Teleoperation module.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Two independent communication paths can be provisioned at the worksite (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual cellular carriers).
Assumption: Failover switchover time is acceptable (typically <100 ms for safety-critical systems).
Risk: If both paths fail simultaneously, availability target cannot be met; mitigation requires geographic/provider diversity.
Risk: Dual-path cost and complexity may exceed budget; trade-off analysis required.
Risk: Latency asymmetry between primary and secondary paths may cause operator confusion; requires transparent UX design.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-B to explicitly specify: (1) failover detection threshold (e.g., packet loss >X% for >Y seconds), (2) switchover latency budget (e.g., <100 ms), (3) path independence criteria (e.g., different carriers, geographic routes), and (4) health monitoring frequency (e.g., heartbeat every Z ms).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the dual-path communication architecture, link health monitoring, failover logic, and operator command/telemetry processing. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (via Tasking and Telemetry) and Safety Services module (via SafetyAlert). It does not own the physical network infrastructure (cellular/satellite carriers) but owns the software logic that selects and switches between available paths.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) transmitted from tele-operator console to Vehicle via Teleoperation module. Dual-path redundancy ensures command delivery.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, system health) transmitted from Vehicle to tele-operator console via Teleoperation module. Dual-path redundancy ensures telemetry reception.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services module notifies Teleoperation of link loss or unsafe conditions; Teleoperation notifies Safety Services of link state changes to trigger safe state transitions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with underlying network infrastructure (primary and secondary communication paths). This is a logical interface; physical realization depends on carrier/technology selection.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of dual-path logic, failover arbitration, and link health monitoring. All core DP-B logic resides here. Minor diffusion: Network Link interface is external (owned by infrastructure), and SafetyAlert coupling requires coordination with Safety Services.

Safety Services could own link loss detection and safe state transition logic (FR-NGHE-010.2), but this would diffuse the communication architecture across two modules. Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle module owns the physical equipment and local control logic, not remote communication. Assigning DP-B to Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle dynamics and network architecture. Not recommended.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained for DP-B ownership. Teleoperation is the appropriate sub-module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-B (dual-path failover architecture) because: (1) it already owns command/telemetry processing, (2) link health monitoring and failover logic are intrinsic to teleoperation, (3) it minimizes diffusion of communication architecture across multiple modules, and (4) it maintains clear separation of concerns (Teleoperation handles remote link; Vehicle handles local control; Safety Services handles safety state).
containment_risks: Risk: If network infrastructure is managed by a separate team/vendor, ownership boundaries may blur. Mitigation: Define clear interface contract (Network Link) between Teleoperation and infrastructure.

Risk: SafetyAlert coupling requires tight coordination between Teleoperation and Safety Services. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert interface specification (message format, latency, reliability) upfront.
Risk: Failover logic complexity may grow if additional paths or technologies are added later. Mitigation: Design Teleoperation module with extensible path abstraction (e.g., pluggable path drivers).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly document the Teleoperation module's responsibilities: (1) manage dual-path selection and switching, (2) monitor link health on both paths, (3) buffer commands/telemetry during brief link transitions, (4) report link state to Safety Services via SafetyAlert, (5) provide operator visibility into link status. Define clear ownership boundary: Teleoperation owns software logic; infrastructure provider owns physical network paths.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (dual-path failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 availability). The logic is sound: redundancy is a proven approach to availability. Assumptions (path independence, failover latency) are reasonable and testable. No fundamental mismatch between requirement and solution concept.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion: Network Link interface is external; Teleoperation depends on infrastructure provider to deliver two independent paths. Mitigation: Define Network Link interface contract clearly.
Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert coupling requires Safety Services to respond to link state changes. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert message specification and latency budget.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score for DP-B. Core failover logic, path selection, and health monitoring are fully contained. External dependencies (Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined interfaces. Ownership is clear and defensible.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability measurement is straightforward: log link state transitions and calculate uptime ratio. Acceptance criterion (≥0.999) is quantitative and testable. Verification can be automated via telemetry logging and post-evaluation analysis.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation module with clear sub-allocations: FR-NGHE-010.1 (link loss detection) → Teleoperation, FR-NGHE-010.2 (safe state transition) → Safety Services (triggered by Teleoperation via SafetyAlert), FR-NGHE-010.3 (redundant paths) → Teleoperation + Infrastructure, FR-NGHE-010.4 (logging) → Teleoperation.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-B specification: add failover detection threshold, switchover latency budget, path independence criteria, and health monitoring frequency.
Define Network Link interface contract: specify path independence requirements, availability SLA for each path, and failover trigger conditions.
Define SafetyAlert interface specification: message format, latency budget, and safe state transition logic.
Document Teleoperation module responsibilities and ownership boundaries explicitly in architecture specification.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (link loss detection, safe state transition, redundant paths, logging). DP-B is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module. However, the sub-FRs require further decomposition to define detailed design parameters (e.g., detection threshold, switchover latency, path selection algorithm). Proceeding to the next level will refine the architecture and reduce risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds (e.g., 100 ms) based on [detection method, e.g., heartbeat timeout or packet loss threshold].
FR-NGHE-010.2: Upon link loss detection, the teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition (e.g., vehicle immobilization or return-to-home) within [Y] milliseconds.

FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain two independent communication paths (primary and secondary) with [path independence criteria, e.g., different carriers, geographic routes] and automatic failover.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions (up/down events, timestamps, path selection) for post-evaluation availability audit.

assumptions: Two independent communication paths can be provisioned at the worksite (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual cellular carriers).
Failover switchover time <100 ms is acceptable for safety-critical teleoperation.
Link health can be monitored via heartbeat or packet loss metrics.
Safety Services module will respond to SafetyAlert notifications within acceptable latency.
Operator console and Vehicle are equipped with dual-path communication hardware.
open_risks: Both communication paths may fail simultaneously if not geographically/provider-diverse; mitigation requires worksite survey and carrier availability analysis.
Dual-path cost and complexity may exceed budget; trade-off analysis required with Project Manager and Site Manager.
Latency asymmetry between primary and secondary paths may cause operator confusion; requires transparent UX design and testing.
SafetyAlert coupling between Teleoperation and Safety Services requires careful interface design to avoid race conditions or deadlocks.
Network infrastructure provider may not guarantee path independence; contractual SLA required.
evidence_needed: Worksite network survey: identify available communication technologies (cellular carriers, satellite providers, WiFi) and geographic coverage.
Carrier SLA documentation: confirm availability guarantees for each path and path independence criteria.
Failover latency measurement: test switchover time under various failure scenarios (packet loss, link down, latency spike).
Safety Services interface specification: define SafetyAlert message format, latency budget, and safe state transition logic.
Operator acceptance testing: validate that dual-path failover is transparent to operator and does not degrade usability.
Availability measurement methodology: define link state logging, data collection, and post-evaluation analysis procedure.
